one_line_takeaway: Introduces the first controlled benchmark (24K multi-turn spoken dialogues with single-turn-localized unsafe content) and shows that LALM safety judges' sensitivity, severity-ordering specificity, and turn-position stability trade off sharply and non-uniformly across model, modality, and transcription source -- no configuration dominates, and even low-WER ASR meaningfully degrades detection.
key_finding: No single judge/modality/transcription combination dominates: under ground-truth transcripts the text-only LLaMA has the highest worst-case Sensitivity (0.457, 95% CI [0.436,0.480]) but also the highest Position Bias for Sensitivity (AAPB_Sens 0.310); the best LALMs peak much lower (Flamingo 0.280 multimodal, Qwen 0.231 multimodal, MERaLiON 0.23 audio-only). Specificity (severity-ordering correctness) is not implied by Sensitivity: LLaMA again leads (0.941) but MERaLiON is close in transcription-only (0.937) and stays high with audio added (0.923), while Qwen's best Specificity requires audio+transcript (0.800) despite modest Sensitivity gains. Modality effects are model-dependent, not universal: adding audio to transcripts raises Qwen's Specificity from 0.667 to 0.800 with almost no Sensitivity change, but for Flamingo audio-only is its weakest setting (Sens 0.154) while transcript-inclusive settings reach 0.28. Even low-WER ASR degrades detection: replacing GT transcripts with Whisper-Large/Base drops LLaMA's Sensitivity from 0.457 to 0.282/0.288 while Specificity stays roughly stable (0.918-0.924), attributed to 'sparse-trigger dependence' where few safety-relevant tokens get erased by minor transcription errors. Masked-audio controls (lexical content destroyed, prosody preserved) show some judges (MERaLiON, Qwen2-Audio) retain non-zero effects, indicating genuine (if model-specific) use of paralinguistic cues rather than pure ASR leakage. TTS synthesis itself introduces a small systematic positive bias (safety scores increase 0.014-0.031 points post-resynthesis) with negligible added WER (<=0.09%). Position Bias and Sensitivity trade off on a Pareto frontier: the lowest-bias configurations cluster at transcription-only Qwen (AAPB_Sens ~0.026-0.029, Sens ~0.221-0.229), while the highest-Sensitivity configuration (LLaMA/GT, Sens 0.457) has the largest AAPB_Sens (0.31).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) an open benchmark of 24,000 multi-turn spoken dialogues, each derived from 100 safe seed dialogues in the DEEPDIALOGUE corpus by replacing a single turn with a GPT-4o-generated unsafe variant (8 categories x 5 severity levels), synthesized to speech with Coqui XTTS-v2 and validated with a 5-rater anonymous human anchor study; (2) a systematic empirical study of 6 large audio-language models (LALMs: Qwen2-Audio, Audio Flamingo 3, MERaLiON, Qwen2.5-Omni, MiniCPM-o-4.5, Gemini-2.5-Flash) plus a text-only LLaMA-3.1-8B baseline acting as zero-shot safety judges across 24+ configurations (judge x modality [audio-only/transcript-only/audio+transcript] x transcription source [GT/Whisper-Large/Whisper-Base]), measured via three novel metrics the authors define -- Sensitivity (worst-case safety-score drop across severities), Specificity (correctness of severity ordering), and Position Bias (dependence of judgments on where the unsafe turn falls in the dialogue); and (3) a practitioner decision flowchart (Fig. 6) distilling model/modality/prompt choices from the empirical results, plus TTS-bias, Whisper-WER, and masked-audio/prosody-control ablations that isolate whether audio effects are lexical (ASR-leakage) or paralinguistic.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the classic sense; this is a safety-evaluation benchmark paper. The 'threat' being modeled is realistic deployment risk: spoken dialogue systems (voice assistants, customer-service agents, tutors, healthcare supporters) that fail to recognize or appropriately respond to harmful content (hate, harassment, dangerous, deception, violence, sexual, self-harm, 'overall' unsafe) delivered via a single turn embedded in an otherwise-safe multi-turn spoken conversation. The paper studies whether automated LALM judges reliably detect such content across modality and transcription-quality conditions, not an attacker actively trying to evade a judge.
limitations: Human anchor study shows a ~15.3% false-positive rate (raters marking safe items as unsafe) and only moderate-to-strong severity agreement on items both GT and rater flag as unsafe (weighted kappa ~0.577-0.80 depending on subset), suggesting some noise/label ambiguity in the ground truth itself. The benchmark is entirely synthetic (GPT-4o-generated unsafe text, TTS-synthesized speech, English only, DEEPDIALOGUE-derived) and does not cover natural spoken safety incidents, multilingual speech, or multiple unsafe turns per dialogue -- authors explicitly flag these as future work. The human study 'does not estimate population-level safety prevalence' per the authors' own caveat. Comparisons rely on only 100 base dialogues (24,000 derived variants are not independent, hence the cluster-bootstrap CI methodology) and 6 LALMs plus one text LLM, so generalization to other/newer model families is unverified. No adversarial robustness analysis is performed -- the paper studies passive detection reliability, not evasion or red-teaming of the judges themselves.

datasets_used: DEEPDIALOGUE (Koudounas et al., 2025) -- source of 100 safe multi-turn synthetic English dialogues, 3-10 turns, filtered with LLaMA Guard
RAVDESS (Livingstone & Russo, 2018) -- reference speaker audio used in DEEPDIALOGUE's original TTS rendering
LALM-as-a-Judge benchmark (this paper's own release) -- 24,000 unsafe spoken dialogue variants across 8 categories x 5 severities, plus human anchor annotations
quantitative_results: Sensitivity (GT transcripts): LLaMA 0.457 [0.436,0.480]; Flamingo (multimodal) 0.280 [0.221,0.337]; Qwen (multimodal) 0.231 [0.211,0.252]; MERaLiON (audio-only) 0.23 [0.215,0.243]

Specificity (GT transcripts): LLaMA 0.941 [0.930,0.953]; MERaLiON (transcript-only) 0.937 [0.919,0.952]; MERaLiON (audio+transcript) 0.923 [0.906,0.938]; Qwen (audio+transcript) 0.800 [0.770,0.827]; Flamingo (transcript-only) 0.824 [0.787,0.857]
Qwen Specificity rises from 0.667 (transcript-only) to 0.800 (multimodal) while Sensitivity changes only 0.229 to 0.231
Whisper transcription effect on LLaMA: Sensitivity drops from 0.457 (GT) to 0.282 (Whisper-Large) / 0.288 (Whisper-Base); Specificity stays 0.918-0.924
Whisper WER: 1.45% (Large) / 3.01% (Base) on safe turns, rising to 1.95% / 5.27% on unsafe turns
TTS-induced bias: safety scores increase 0.014-0.031 points after resynthesis, averaged across 5 prompting strategies; added WER only 0.01-0.09%

Human anchor study: Cohen's kappa for binary safety = 0.836 +/-0.06; unsafe-content recall (TPR) = 97.2% +/-1.3%; false-positive rate on safe items = 15.3% +/-7.7%; severity weighted kappa = 0.797 (all) / 0.577 (GT-intersect-unsafe subset); Spearman rho = 0.590; within-+/-1-severity-level accuracy = 83.5%; Krippendorff's alpha = 0.849 (safety) / 0.923 (severity)

Category-level Specificity gains from audio: hate multimodal ~0.71 vs transcript-only ~0.54; deception ~0.76 vs ~0.61; audio-only/multimodal beat transcript-only Specificity in 6/8 and 7/8 categories respectively
Position Bias: LLaMA AAPB_Sens 0.310 (GT); Qwen multimodal AAPB_Sens 0.294; Qwen transcript-only AAPB_Sens ~0.029; Flamingo multimodal AAPB_Spec ~0.016; MERaLiON audio-only AAPB_Spec ~0.037

Prompting-strategy swing: up to 0.4 absolute range in Sensitivity and 0.27 in Specificity across the 5 strategies tested; Sens-optimal prompts were few-shot in 9/24 configs, CoT in 7/24, basic in 5/24, calibrated in 3/24; Spec-optimal split 9/24 rubric, 9/24 basic, 3/24 few-shot, 3/24 CoT
